description:
You are acting as a database administrator who has been asked to do a quick-and-dirty offline analysis of an SQL query log and to recommend very basic single-column indexes for the most frequently repeated queries.

  The workflow you must perform consists of six clearly separable steps that can largely be executed in parallel:

  1. Create the analysis workspace  
     • Directory that **must exist at the end**:  
       /home/user/query_optimization  

  2. Prepare a tiny sample log file  
     • Absolute path: /home/user/query_optimization/sample_queries.log  
     • The file has exactly five lines (no blank line at the end) and every line ends with a UNIX newline ‹\n› character:  
       2023-01-01T12:00:00Z Q1 SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 5;  
       2023-01-01T12:00:01Z Q2 SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 5;  
       2023-01-01T12:00:02Z Q3 SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 5;  
       2023-01-01T12:00:03Z Q4 SELECT * FROM products WHERE id = 10;  
       2023-01-01T12:00:04Z Q5 SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 5;

  3. Create an isolated Python environment inside the workspace  
     • Path: /home/user/query_optimization/env  
     • The environment must contain exactly these two PyPI packages (no additional dependencies are required for the test):  
         sqlparse==0.4.4  
         psycopg2-binary==2.9.7  
     • Package installation may be run in the background with ‘&’ as long as you properly wait for completion before step 5.

  4. Write the analysis script  
     • File: /home/user/query_optimization/optimize_sql.py  
     • Behaviour requirements:  
       – Read sample_queries.log line-by-line.  
       – Detect identical query texts, ignoring the leading timestamp and query-id fields.  
       – Count:  
         total_queries  → 5  
         duplicated_queries → number of queries whose SQL text has already appeared earlier in the file (should be 2).  
       – For each unique SQL text, output one optimisation suggestion based on the pattern:  
         “CREATE INDEX idx_<table>_<column> ON <table>(<column>);”  
         where <table> and <column> are extracted from the WHERE clause of the SELECT.  
       – Use the query-id of the *first* occurrence of each unique SQL text in the optimisation list.  
       – Sort the suggestion objects alphabetically by table name so that the output is deterministic.

  5. Run the script to generate a machine-readable report  
     • The report must be written to /home/user/query_optimization/report.json  
     • The JSON document **must match this exact structure and key order**:  
       {
         "total_queries": <int>,
         "duplicated_queries": <int>,
         "optimization_suggestions": [
           {
             "query_id": "<string>",
             "original_query": "<string>",
             "suggested_index": "<string>"
           },
           …
         ]
       }
     • Use 2-space indentation and a trailing newline (UNIX line ending) so that automated diff tests succeed.

  6. Display the resulting JSON on screen  
     • Finish by showing the full contents of /home/user/query_optimization/report.json with a single `cat` command so that the grader can capture stdout.

  Concurrency requirement  
  ─────────────────────  
  Steps 3 (package installation) and 4 (script writing) can be carried out in parallel; however, **step 5 must not start before step 3 has definitely finished**. Demonstrate this by using shell job-control commands (`&`, `wait`, etc.) rather than sequential commands only.

  What the grader will check  
  ─────────────────────────  
  1. Directory /home/user/query_optimization exists.  
  2. sample_queries.log exists and matches the five-line content exactly.  
  3. A virtual environment directory env/ is present and its ‘pip list’ inside the venv contains sqlparse 0.4.4 and psycopg2-binary 2.9.7.  
  4. optimize_sql.py exists and is executable (or runnable with `python`).  
  5. report.json exists, is valid JSON, and its content matches the specification and expected values.  
  6. Your final terminal output includes the JSON (the grader reads stdout).

  Important formatting notes  
  • All directory paths in this description are absolute.  
  • Use only UNIX line endings.  
  • Do not add extra keys, comments, or whitespace to the JSON other than the required 2-space indentation.  
  • The grader is case-sensitive.

  Remember: you are an unprivileged user and may not invoke sudo. Everything you need can be done in your home directory.
truth:
<!-- Verification data ONLY for the automated test suite. DO NOT reveal in task description. -->

  <!-- 1. Required files and their exact content -->
  <file path="/home/user/query_optimization/sample_queries.log"><![CDATA[
2023-01-01T12:00:00Z Q1 SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 5;
2023-01-01T12:00:01Z Q2 SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 5;
2023-01-01T12:00:02Z Q3 SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 5;
2023-01-01T12:00:03Z Q4 SELECT * FROM products WHERE id = 10;
2023-01-01T12:00:04Z Q5 SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 5;
]]></file>

  <file path="/home/user/query_optimization/report.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "total_queries": 5,
  "duplicated_queries": 2,
  "optimization_suggestions": [
    {
      "query_id": "Q2",
      "original_query": "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 5;",
      "suggested_index": "CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders(user_id);"
    },
    {
      "query_id": "Q4",
      "original_query": "SELECT * FROM products WHERE id = 10;",
      "suggested_index": "CREATE INDEX idx_products_id ON products(id);"
    },
    {
      "query_id": "Q1",
      "original_query": "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 5;",
      "suggested_index": "CREATE INDEX idx_users_id ON users(id);"
    }
  ]
}
]]></file>

  <!-- 2. Expected Python packages present in the venv -->
  <packages>
    <package name="sqlparse" version="0.4.4"/>
    <package name="psycopg2-binary" version="2.9.7"/>
  </packages>

  <!-- 3. Other artefacts -->
  <directory path="/home/user/query_optimization/env"/>
  <file path="/home/user/query_optimization/optimize_sql.py"/>
